paragraphs.text: eu travel tech
EU TRAVEL TECH POSITION PAPER

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: eu travel tech, representing leading travel intermediaries, welcomes the European Commission's efforts to improve consumer rights in the digital environment. Consumer protection, trust and transparency are inherent priorities of companies in the travel tech sector, who remain committed to continue upholding the high consumer protection standards that the EU prides itself on. It is against this background that we provide our response to the Commission's consultation below.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: The EU already has a comprehensive body of law (some of it only recently enacted or subject to ongoing revision) covering the issues raised in the consultation, including the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD), the Consumer Rights Directive (CRD), the Digital Services Act (DSA), the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), the AI Act, and sectoral regimes such as the Package Travel Directive (PTD) and the Air Services Regulation (ASR). These instruments are technologically neutral and principle-based, allowing them to flexibly capture new digital practices without requiring constant legislative updates.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: creating regulatory overlap and complexity
fragmented enforcement
diverging national interpretations
inconsistent application of existing rules
legal uncertainty
distort competition
erode consumer trust.

paragraphs.text: We are concerned that introducing additional legislative layers risks duplicating existing obligations, creating regulatory overlap and complexity, particularly for businesses that operate across the EU. Instead, where we see a need for improvement is the fragmented enforcement, diverging national interpretations, and inconsistent application of existing rules, all of which create legal uncertainty, distort competition, and erode consumer trust.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: coordinated and harmonised enforcement of consumer and other relevant rules across the EU.
Where needed, guidance to clarify the application of rules and the interplay between laws would be valuable.

paragraphs.text: Against this backdrop, eu travel tech would suggest a shift of focus to measures enabling more coordinated and harmonised enforcement of consumer and other relevant rules across the EU. Where needed, guidance to clarify the application of rules and the interplay between laws would be valuable.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: interface design
pricing transparency in accommodation and flight ticket sales

paragraphs.text: Our recommendations draw directly on our extensive experience in the travel tech sector. Coordinated enforcement, such as past CPC joint actions on interface design and pricing transparency in accommodation and flight ticket sales, has yielded rapid and lasting compliance improvements. Conversely, vague or

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: overlapping obligations have resulted in inconsistent application, legal uncertainty, and competitive distortions. We believe that there is room to build on the success of the CPC Regulation to increase the legal certainty that comes with the coordinated and joint nature of decisions.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: Overall, we urge EU policymakers to focus on the three mutually reinforcing pillars:

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: Guidance to clarify the interplay of different rules to ensure consistent enforcement of existing EU consumer law across Member States;

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: Coordinated enforcement between Member States and various authorities;

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: Harmonised enforcement mechanisms with proportionality and safe harbour incentives for responsible businesses.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: We set out below detailed comments on the sections of the Commission's consultation that are the most relevant to our members to further elaborate on our responses to the questionnaire.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: Dark patterns: Darks patterns are already regulated (and banned) by different pieces of EU legislation covering the topic from various perspectives.

paragraphs.text: Dark patterns: Darks patterns are already regulated (and banned) by different pieces of EU legislation covering the topic from various perspectives. In our view, further legislative action in this area is unnecessary and risks being disproportionate by banning legitimate marketing practices such as availability messages or use of colours and fonts. The Commission should consider updating existing guidelines and complement them, where necessary, with guidance clarifying how existing legal instruments (UCPD, CRD, DSA, DMA, GDPR, AI Act, Data Act) can coherently apply at the same time, with practical and clear do's and don'ts and sectoral-relevant, non-exhaustive examples of what is permissible. Enforcement of existing laws should be prioritised rather than new rigid blacklists prone to over-reach and rapid obsolescence. A shared best-practice repository could promote effective compliance without adding new legal layers.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: an addictive design may constitute an unfair commercial practice under the UCPD

paragraphs.text: Addictive design: A blanket ban on mechanisms deemed as potentially addictive would be too restrictive, as it would disproportionately curtail legitimate design strategies without necessarily leading to a higher level of consumer protection. Designs that, under certain circumstances, produce certain behavioural effects are already regulated under existing EU legal instruments and can be sanctioned accordingly. For example, an addictive design may constitute an unfair commercial practice under the UCPD if it negatively influences consumers' transactional decisions, or may lead to GDPR sanctions if it involves a violation of data protection law when processing personal data. Consistent enforcement of these rules would provide a better path to greater digital fairness. In addition, travel is an infrequent product. It

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: "addiction"

paragraphs.text: naturally does not lend itself to "addiction" or "excessive use." We therefore urge the Commission to be aware of unintended consequences from introducing broadly applicable principles and requirements in this area: different business models exist and the nature of various products and services should be taken into account. Where there are particular concerns with certain types of users and services, the Commission should consider targeted interventions rather than new horizontal rules.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: Personalisation is already comprehensively regulated under the UCPD, CRD, GDPR, DSA, DMA and the AI Act, covering both consumer-facing and data-processing aspects.

paragraphs.text: Personalisation: Personalisation is already comprehensively regulated under the UCPD, CRD, GDPR, DSA, DMA and the AI Act, covering both consumer-facing and data-processing aspects. There is therefore no need for additional legislative action. The challenge lies in fragmented interpretation and a lack of clarity in how these laws interact with each other, not legislative gaps. Crucially, authorities should not confuse dynamic pricing with discriminatory personalised pricing and explicitly preserve lawful supply/demand-driven (dynamic) pricing and lawful personalisation (such as when used to offer discounts to certain groups) as legitimate practices when in line with existing legislation. Additional harmonised cross-regulatory guidance (e.g. EDPB in coordination with relevant consumer protection authorities or DSA coordinators) is critical to clarify how existing laws interact with one another and avoid conflicting obligations and fragmentation across consumer, data protection, and AI legal regimes. The recently published guidelines on the interplay between the GDPR and the DSA are a positive development in this regard, and we would encourage such guidelines in other areas.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: there are many legitimate applications of dynamic pricing
a blanket ban would be disproportionate and unfair to consumers
There are many different forms of dynamic pricing and their application varies in industries and business models.

paragraphs.text: Unfair marketing related to pricing: Existing rules (notably the UCPD, CRD and Price Indications Directive) already mandate pricing transparency and require traders to communicate the total price to the consumer at the point of sale or earlier. In addition, the Air Services Regulation (currently under revision) sets specific transparency requirements for air fares. The priority should be to ensure a more coordinated and harmonised enforcement, rather than to adopt additional legislation. As mentioned above, there are many legitimate applications of dynamic pricing and a blanket ban would be disproportionate and unfair to consumers, who benefit from lower prices when demand is low. It should be kept in mind that there are many different forms of dynamic pricing and their application varies in industries and business models.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: "subscription traps"
to cancel a contract or subscription online in just a few steps, without having to navigate hidden menus or contact customer service.
manipulative interface designs

paragraphs.text: Digital contracts: Existing legislation already protects consumers from "subscription traps". The Consumer Rights Directive (last amended in 2023) includes a requirement for traders to provide consumers with a clear and easily accessible online mechanism (such as a button or similar tool) to cancel a contract or subscription online in just a few steps, without having to navigate hidden menus or contact customer service. The UCPD treats onerous and disproportionate barriers to termination as aggressive practices as well as the DSA which prohibits manipulative interface designs that affect the

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: 2 By the Directive (EU) 2023/2673 adopted in November 2023 which must be transposed by Member States into their national law by 19 December 2025 and will apply from 19 June 2026.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: Free trials provide clear consumer benefits and are already regulated under the CRD and UCPD.

paragraphs.text: ability to make free and informed decisions. Free trials provide clear consumer benefits and are already regulated under the CRD and UCPD. Over-restricting them could reduce availability and consumer choice. Requiring payment details upfront can prevent abuse, verify age, and ensure consumers are fully aware of any post-trial payment obligation. Any action in this area should preserve justified sectoral nuances (e.g., non-refundable dated travel bookings).

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: Instead of an overhaul of the EU consumer acquis for the digital environment, there is an opportunity to streamline overlapping pre-contractual and interface disclosure obligations into concise, intuitive, layered "key facts" to reduce redundancy and cognitive overload.

Digital-by-default formats, such as expandable sections, icons, and inline highlights, should be encouraged over dense legacy text, while ensuring consumers receive durable copies of terms and conditions.
layered "key facts" to reduce redundancy and cognitive overload.
repetitive transactions with the same supplier (such as in-app purchases)
and automated contracts concluded by consumers using a digital (AI) assistant should be reduced.

paragraphs.text: Simplification and fragmentation: Given the recent regulatory wave (DSA, DMA, AI Act, etc), we believe that it is important to allow time for these new rules to take root and show effects before considering additional legislation. Where needed, practical guidance would enable effective and consistent implementation between Member States, avoid unnecessary burdens on businesses, and more closely align with the EU's efforts to streamline regulation and boost the competitiveness of its economy. Instead of an overhaul of the EU consumer acquis for the digital environment, there is an opportunity to streamline overlapping pre-contractual and interface disclosure obligations into concise, intuitive, layered "key facts" to reduce redundancy and cognitive overload. Digital-by-default formats, such as expandable sections, icons, and inline highlights, should be encouraged over dense legacy text, while ensuring consumers receive durable copies of terms and conditions. Information requirements under the CRD in respect of repetitive transactions with the same supplier (such as in-app purchases) and automated contracts concluded by consumers using a digital (AI) assistant should be reduced.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: Horizontal enforcement and coherence: The European Commission should prioritise coordinated enforcement of existing rules, continue CPC joint actions and formalise structured cooperation among consumer protection and data protection authorities, DSA coordinators, and relevant Commission services to deliver consistent, non-duplicative remedies where needed. It should also introduce proportionate safe harbour incentives for responsible/cooperative traders and avoid broad new definitions that would undermine legal certainty. Concepts such as 'average' and 'vulnerable' consumers, the reversal of the burden of proof, or the introduction of 'fairness by design' should be carefully evaluated in light of their potential impact on legal predictability and the EU's competitiveness.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: In summary, eu travel tech is concerned that an update of the EU consumer acquis with a DFA will accentuate the complexity of the existing legal framework and increase compliance costs for businesses such as eu travel tech members. If regulation is put forward, it should be targeted and proportionate to the level of risk of consumer harm. This is important to limit unintended consequences and avoid increasing compliance burdens for businesses with not much incremental consumer benefit. Guidelines in certain areas could help to clarify the application and interaction of laws and enable a more coordinated and harmonised

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: enforcement. We stand ready to collaborate with the European Commission and other stakeholders to operationalise these goals with practical expertise.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: Personalised search results, dynamic recommendations, and clear presentation of offers save consumers time and make travel planning more efficient.

paragraphs.text: The digital transformation of travel has fundamentally changed how consumers research, compare, book, and manage their travel journeys. Travel tech platforms help travellers navigate vast and complex information on flights, accommodations, experiences, add-ons, insurance, and membership benefits, enabling them to quickly find relevant options tailored to their preferences and needs. Personalised search results, dynamic recommendations, and clear presentation of offers save consumers time and make travel planning more efficient.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: transparent user interfaces
accurate pricing information
clear contractual terms

paragraphs.text: Consumer trust in this ecosystem depends on transparent user interfaces, accurate pricing information, a relevant and smooth consumer experience, and clear contractual terms.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: user-centred personalisation

paragraphs.text: As participants in a sector that relies almost entirely on consumer trust and willingness to compare and find the best offers, travel tech companies see transparent display and user-centred personalisation implemented in line with existing legislation as key competitive advantages. While a small number of opaque or abusive practices can undermine confidence, the vast majority of actors in the sector provide services that empower consumers and facilitate better, faster, and more informed travel choices.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: data use
algorithmic decision-making

paragraphs.text: The EU has progressively built a comprehensive, multi-layered digital and consumer protection framework. A range of legal instruments sets out transparency, fairness, and accountability requirements across commercial practices, data use, platform responsibilities, and algorithmic decision-making. Together, these legal instruments form the legal foundation for many of the issues addressed in this paper. Specific provisions are referenced in the relevant thematic sections below.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: From our experience, significant enforcement shortcomings remain, with authorities often acting in an uncoordinated manner and approaching the same issues from different angles. These challenges, also highlighted in the European

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: fragmented enforcement across Member States
overlapping or unclear obligations that complicate compliance
diverging national interpretations that fragment the Single Market and drive up compliance costs

paragraphs.text: Commission’s Fitness Check on digital fairness, include fragmented enforcement across Member States, overlapping or unclear obligations that complicate compliance, and diverging national interpretations that fragment the Single Market and drive up compliance costs.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: eu travel tech believes the European Commission, working with Member States under the existing legal framework, should focus on promoting more coordinated and consistent cross-border enforcement and, where needed, providing practical guidance on existing law, rather than introducing an additional legal layer in a regulatory landscape which already contains extensive consumer protections. In our view, there is no clear evidence of legal gaps, and it makes little sense to introduce new legal obligations without first improving enforcement of existing ones. Rather, consistent and coordinated enforcement, if applied effectively, will enable authorities to tackle the remaining consumer protection challenges.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: intentionally mislead consumers

paragraphs.text: User interface designs are legitimate business practices, except when they fail to comply with existing legal requirements and thereby intentionally mislead consumers to make decisions they would not have taken otherwise. Without this distinction, virtually all offers, marketing and advertising measures would risk being treated as unlawful.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: Deceptive design can distort consumer behaviour by prompting people to act contrary to their genuine status and undermine trust in businesses when used in a manipulative or misleading manner.

paragraphs.text: Deceptive design can distort consumer behaviour by prompting people to act contrary to their genuine status and undermine trust in businesses when used in a manipulative or misleading manner. At the same time, legitimate user-experience features, such as the clear display of genuine popularity or reliability indicators, or real availability limitations, should remain permissible as deemed helpful by consumers in making their travel plans.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: The existing EU legal framework already captures many dark patterns:

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT
2.2. Existing legal coverage and enforcement lessons

evidence_quotes.quote: The UCPD’s general clause prohibits unfair commercial practices that distort consumer behaviour

paragraphs.text: The UCPD’s general clause prohibits unfair commercial practices that distort consumer behaviour, and Annex I already lists specific prohibited techniques. Taken together, this empowers enforcement authorities to tackle both

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT
2.2. Existing legal coverage and enforcement lessons

paragraphs.text: established and emerging manipulative interface designs without requiring new legislative measures.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT
2.2. Existing legal coverage and enforcement lessons

evidence_quotes.quote: unauthorised addition of items into an online shopping basket ("sneaking")

paragraphs.text: The CRD, particularly Art. 6 on information obligations and Art. 8 on the formal requirements for distance contracts, prevents material information from being obscured or ambiguous. Moreover, Art. 8(2), which sets out requirements for the labelling of purchase buttons, aims to ensure that consumers are clearly informed when they are entering into a binding contract that entails a payment obligation. This is complemented by Art. 22 which mandates express consent (i.e., opt-in without preselection) for any additional costs. Art. 27 also exempts consumers from any obligation to provide consideration (i.e. make a payment) in cases of unsolicited supply or provision and specifies that a lack of response following such unsolicited supply does not constitute consent. This provision is directly relevant to practices such as the unauthorised addition of items into an online shopping basket ("sneaking").

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT
2.2. Existing legal coverage and enforcement lessons

evidence_quotes.quote: The GDPR and the EDPB guidelines on dark patterns prohibit deceptive consent practices and opaque user interfaces that obscure data processing.

paragraphs.text: The GDPR and the EDPB guidelines on dark patterns prohibit deceptive consent practices and opaque user interfaces that obscure data processing. Consent must be freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous, and users must receive clear, accessible information about how their personal data is used. Interface tactics that pressure users into consenting or conceal the true nature of data processing may violate these requirements.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT
2.2. Existing legal coverage and enforcement lessons

evidence_quotes.quote: The DSA prohibits online platforms from designing, organising or operating their interfaces in ways that deceive or materially distort users' ability to make free, informed choices.
must offer at least one recommendation option that is not based on profiling.

paragraphs.text: The DSA prohibits online platforms from designing, organising or operating their interfaces in ways that deceive or materially distort users' ability to make free, informed choices. Providers of online platforms must clearly explain their recommender systems' main parameters and provide users controls to adjust those settings wherever several options are available, while very large online platforms (VLOPs) and search engines (VLSEs) must offer at least one recommendation option that is not based on profiling. Together, these rules help curb manipulative design choices and empower users with transparency and genuine alternatives.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT
2.2. Existing legal coverage and enforcement lessons

evidence_quotes.quote: the use of dark patterns to unfairly influence consumer decisions.

paragraphs.text: The DMA, in particular Art. 5(2), obliges gatekeepers to ensure that withdrawing consent is as simple as granting it, and Art. 13, which prohibits designated gatekeepers from circumventing the DMA's obligations through contractual, commercial, technical, or other means, also addresses the use of dark patterns to unfairly influence consumer decisions.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT
2.2. Existing legal coverage and enforcement lessons

evidence_quotes.quote: The AI Act, particularly Art. 5(1)(a) and (b), prohibits subliminal, manipulative or deceptive techniques, including those that exploit vulnerabilities of specific groups of persons where they materially distort behaviour and cause, or are likely to cause, harm.

paragraphs.text: The AI Act, particularly Art. 5(1)(a) and (b), prohibits subliminal, manipulative or deceptive techniques, including those that exploit vulnerabilities of specific groups of persons where they materially distort behaviour and cause, or are likely to cause, harm.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT
2.2. Existing legal coverage and enforcement lessons

paragraphs.text: There are also non-regulatory measures in place, such as:

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT
2.2. Existing legal coverage and enforcement lessons

paragraphs.text: 5 Art. 25 DSA 6 Art. 27 DSA 7 Art. 29 DSA

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT
2.2. Existing legal coverage and enforcement lessons

evidence_quotes.quote: practices falling under the notion of dark patterns.

paragraphs.text: The Commission's Notice on the interpretation and application of the UCPD, which includes a dedicated section (Section 4.2.7.) on how the regulatory framework on unfair commercial practices applies to practices falling under the notion of dark patterns. As noted in this section: "The UCPD applies to any 'unfair commercial practice' that meets the requirements of the material scope of the Directive, regardless of their classification. If dark patterns are applied in the context of business-to-consumer commercial relationships, then the Directive can be used to challenge the fairness of such practices, in addition to other instruments in the EU legal framework, such as the GDPR."

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT
2.2. Existing legal coverage and enforcement lessons

evidence_quotes.quote: deceptive design patterns in social media platform interfaces

paragraphs.text: Guidelines issued by the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) in 2022 on deceptive design patterns in social media platform interfaces, which clarify how such practices are assessed under the GDPR.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT
2.2. Existing legal coverage and enforcement lessons

evidence_quotes.quote: include all mandatory fees in initial price displays, when known and calculable at the time of booking.
availability messages
"popularity" indicators

paragraphs.text: Targeted enforcement by the CPC has demonstrated the practical efficiency of these rules when applied uniformly. For example, in 2019-2020, eu travel tech members voluntarily committed to adjust certain marketing practices. In particular, certain online travel platforms made commitments that established best practices in relation to availability messages, discount harmonisation and "popularity" indicators, and committed to include all mandatory fees in initial price displays, when known and calculable at the time of booking.8 These coordinated interventions demonstrate that targeted, cross-border enforcement using the existing legal framework paired with clear expectations can swiftly drive compliance, align platform practices with legal norms, and materially enhance consumer fairness.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT
2.2. Existing legal coverage and enforcement lessons

paragraphs.text: eu travel tech supports the following key principles for addressing dark patterns:

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: Avoid prescriptive interface design rules (e.g., mandating exact colours or button shapes)

paragraphs.text: Principles-based framing: Avoid prescriptive interface design rules (e.g., mandating exact colours or button shapes) that risk hindering businesses' ability to differentiate their products and marketing and may quickly become obsolete or be easily circumvented. Instead, the focus should be on the coordinated and consistent enforcement and interpretation of existing law.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: genuinely manipulative tactics

paragraphs.text: Contextual differentiation: Clearly distinguish genuinely manipulative tactics from legitimate interface features aiding navigation or decision-making (e.g., highlighting popular options or using availability messages, or communicating the benefits of a contract).

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: Legal clarity: Provide non-legislative guidance with practical examples under existing rules (UCPD, GDPR, DSA, etc.), to reduce divergence in national

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: 8 CPC coordinated action (2019–2020) leading to interface and pricing improvements on major online travel booking platforms.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: enforcement and legal uncertainty, while avoiding new rigid definitions or legislative blacklists.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: Harm-based prioritisation: Prioritise a targeted and proportionate approach over blanket restrictions. There is a significant variety of business models online with different challenges presented for each business and each industry sector. Taking into account the level of risk of consumer harm would be a more proportionate approach.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: Regulating dark patterns

paragraphs.text: Case-by-case assessment: Regulating dark patterns should aim to enhance consumer protection without hindering mutually beneficial interactions between consumers and traders. Accordingly, the assessment of whether a practice is harmful or unfair should be conducted on a case-by-case basis, taking into account the specific context, intent, and effects of the interaction.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: To operationalise these principles, we recommend:

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: apply to dark patterns

paragraphs.text: Harmonised guidance: The Commission may publish guidance clarifying how existing rules (such as the UCPD, DSA and GDPR) apply to dark patterns. This guidance could provide illustrative "do's and don'ts" examples to help businesses clearly distinguish harmful deceptive patterns from lawful design and prioritise coordinated enforcement in high-harm areas, such as gambling and child-facing interfaces. Vague definitions and new legislative blacklists should be avoided.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: best practices in transparent design

paragraphs.text: Safe harbour incentives: Encourage adoption of best practices in transparent design (e.g., informative and accurate messaging about limitations in supply and availability) by offering presumptions of compliance benefits to businesses having demonstrated increased degree of cooperation.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: Flexible enforcement mechanisms: Enable enforcement authorities to respond quickly to new or evolving dark patterns by relying on guidance as the interpretative anchor, thus reducing the need for frequent legislative updates, and leveraging existing CPC joint actions and sweeps.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: Promote combined actions when issues cut across multiple legal domains

paragraphs.text: Joint investigations: Promote combined actions when issues cut across multiple legal domains to prevent conflicting remedies or litigation uncertainty and ensure coherent outcomes across consumer, data protection and DSA enforcement authorities.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: Collaboration: Collaborate with industry on guidance, while promoting convergence on best practices across Member States.

section_path: EC CONSULTATION ON DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: Personalisation in travel, such as tailoring search results, pricing, recommendations, or advertising based on past behaviour, loyalty preferences, or inferred profiles, can significantly improve relevance, discovery, and consumer satisfaction.

paragraphs.text: Personalisation in travel, such as tailoring search results, pricing, recommendations, or advertising based on past behaviour, loyalty preferences, or inferred profiles, can significantly improve relevance, discovery, and consumer satisfaction. Examples include ranking results based on airline or hotel loyalty memberships, or prioritising family-friendly options for users who previously searched for family travel.

section_path: 3.1. Balancing personalisation benefits and consumer protection

evidence_quotes.quote: Personalised advertising and personalised pricing are strategic imperatives for modern businesses of all sizes.

paragraphs.text: Personalised advertising and personalised pricing are strategic imperatives for modern businesses of all sizes. By tailoring offers and marketing efforts to individual customer behaviours, businesses can shift from broad outreach to precise engagement, reaching the consumers most likely to be receptive. This enhanced efficiency ensures that every marketing euro is spent wisely, allowing for a more strategic allocation of resources and strengthening competitiveness.

section_path: 3.1. Balancing personalisation benefits and consumer protection

evidence_quotes.quote: personalisation delivers relevance, convenience, and value.

paragraphs.text: From the consumer perspective, personalisation delivers relevance, convenience, and value. By surfacing products and services aligned with past behaviour and expressed interests, it helps consumers discover new options they genuinely want or need, acting as a curated guide to the marketplace. This not only saves time and effort but also fosters a more satisfying and trusting relationship between consumers and businesses. It can also deliver broader social value, for example by facilitating accessible travel options for persons with reduced mobility, tailoring offers to specific demographic needs or pointing consumers to less visited destinations that match their previous preferences.

section_path: 3.1. Balancing personalisation benefits and consumer protection

evidence_quotes.quote: consumers no longer simply appreciate personalisation.
with 88% of consumers more likely to make a purchase when brands personalise interactions in real time.

paragraphs.text: Twilio's 2025 State of Customer Engagement Report shows that consumers no longer simply appreciate personalisation. They now expect it as a basic utility and its absence is a major driver for abandonment, with 88% of consumers more likely to make a purchase when brands personalise interactions in real time. Consumers expect brands to engage them "in the moment, not hours or days later," and personalized engagement is a critical factor in purchase decisions for 64% of consumers.

section_path: 3.1. Balancing personalisation benefits and consumer protection

evidence_quotes.quote: Personalisation should not be considered inherently unfair.

paragraphs.text: Personalisation should not be considered inherently unfair. Platforms must retain the ability to tailor offers and communications, including those directed at specific groups, provided these practices are transparent, respect consumer autonomy, and are not exploitative. For example, offering a 20% discount on city tours to travellers with reduced mobility should clearly qualify as lawful and beneficial practice.

section_path: 3.1. Balancing personalisation benefits and consumer protection

paragraphs.text: There is currently no clear evidence of a legislative gap in the area. The priority should therefore be the consistent application and enforcement of existing rules, rather than introducing new legislation.

section_path: 3.1. Balancing personalisation benefits and consumer protection

evidence_quotes.quote: Personalisation is regulated under multiple EU legal regimes

paragraphs.text: Personalisation is regulated under multiple EU legal regimes, creating complexity for travel tech providers seeking to comply coherently across different legal requirements:

section_path: 3.2. Fragmented expectations across EU legal instruments

evidence_quotes.quote: The UCPD captures misleading or unduly influential personalised commercial practices,
prices are personalised based on profiling.

paragraphs.text: UCPD and Omnibus Directive: The UCPD captures misleading or unduly influential personalised commercial practices, while the Omnibus Directive mandates disclosure when prices are personalised based on profiling.

section_path: 3.2. Fragmented expectations across EU legal instruments

paragraphs.text: GDPR: Grants individuals rights related to automated decision-making and restricts profiling absent lawful, transparent, and fair basis.

section_path: 3.2. Fragmented expectations across EU legal instruments

evidence_quotes.quote: explain how recommender systems function
non-profiled alternatives
personalised advertising based on special categories of personal data (such as racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religious beliefs, or health data).

paragraphs.text: DSA: Requires platforms to explain how recommender systems function and to offer non-profiled alternatives. It also prohibits personalised advertising based on special categories of personal data (such as racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religious beliefs, or health data).

section_path: 3.2. Fragmented expectations across EU legal instruments

evidence_quotes.quote: including those used for personalisation.

paragraphs.text: AI Act: Will impose additional obligations on high-risk AI systems that materially affect individuals, including those used for personalisation.

section_path: 3.2. Fragmented expectations across EU legal instruments

paragraphs.text: Travel tech firms often face tensions in reconciling these overlapping obligations. The industry would benefit from harmonised, cross-regulatory guidance to align expectations across consumer protection, data protection and AI legal regimes and support consistent compliance. This is particularly important to ensure that enforcement authorities adopt coherent approaches and that businesses are not faced with contradictory requirements across different regimes.

section_path: 3.2. Fragmented expectations across EU legal instruments

paragraphs.text: To address these challenges, we urge for:

evidence_quotes.quote: travel-personalisation scenarios

paragraphs.text: Harmonised inter-regulatory guidance: Develop a joint, cross-regime compliance guidance that unifies expectations across consumer protection, data protection law, and algorithmic fairness frameworks, notably in travel-personalisation scenarios, to reduce confusion and conflicting interpretations.

evidence_quotes.quote: personalisation practices implicate multiple legal regimes

paragraphs.text: Coordinate enforcement and joint investigations: Encourage joint investigations and actions among relevant enforcement authorities when personalisation practices implicate multiple legal regimes, to avoid fragmented remedies, duplicative processes, or inconsistent outcomes. Resources should focus on clearly non-compliant actors to maximise
consumer benefit while minimising unnecessary burdens on compliant businesses.

Price transparency is one of the defining strengths of the travel tech sector. Digital intermediaries have transformed how consumers access and compare offers, creating an environment where clear, real-time pricing empowers travellers and fosters competition between suppliers. This dynamic has made travel one of the most transparent and consumer-driven sectors in the digital economy.

evidence_quotes.quote: practices that enable consumers to find the right offer at the right price

paragraphs.text: For this transparency to continue delivering value, the regulatory framework should support, not disrupt, practices that enable consumers to find the right offer at the right price. The challenge is therefore not a lack of rules, but ensuring that existing obligations are applied consistently, proportionately, and with a clear understanding of how the travel ecosystem works.

This requires a policy approach that recognises the positive contribution of travel technology to consumer choice, market efficiency, and fair competition, while addressing harmful practices in a targeted way.

evidence_quotes.quote: abusive pricing practices

paragraphs.text: Strengthen enforcement against abusive pricing practices: Apply existing legal instruments (UCPD, Omnibus Directive and the Air Services Regulation).

evidence_quotes.quote: unfair individualised price discrimination.
opaque profiling mechanisms.

paragraphs.text: Preserve legitimate dynamic pricing: Explicitly distinguish demand-driven dynamic pricing from unfair individualised price discrimination. Dynamic pricing reflecting market conditions should remain permissible, provided variability is communicated transparently and not obscured through opaque profiling mechanisms. Dynamic pricing should be expressly recognised as legitimate in guidance to avoid chilling effects on pro-consumer, market-reflective practices.

evidence_quotes.quote: Harmonisation of price presentation should be understood as alignment of disclosure and display rules, not as an obligation to ensure identical prices across distribution channels.

paragraphs.text: Promote consistency of price presentation without regulatory duplication: As Article 23 of the Air Services Regulation is currently under revision, avoid creating overlapping legal layers. Instead, promote convergence through guidance on the interplay between laws and coordinated CPC actions to reduce fragmentation and compliance costs across Member States. Harmonisation of price presentation should be understood as alignment of disclosure and display rules, not as an obligation to ensure identical prices across distribution channels. For instance, hotel prices displayed on

evidence_quotes.quote: What matters is that all prices are displayed in a transparent, comparable, and consistent way.

paragraphs.text: metasearch platforms and on hotel websites may legitimately differ. What matters is that all prices are displayed in a transparent, comparable, and consistent way.

evidence_quotes.quote: Reference prices used in discount claims should be verifiable against the traders' previous prices

paragraphs.text: Enforce truthful discounting: Reference prices used in discount claims should be verifiable against the traders' previous prices, recent, and reflect a genuine previous price; not artificially inflated baselines designed to exaggerate savings. Encourage clear labelling of the reference price used (e.g., RRP, manufacturer's suggested price).

evidence_quotes.quote: optional ancillary services

paragraphs.text: Preserve consumer choice in ancillaries: Ensure enforcement and guidance explicitly safeguard consumers' freedom to purchase optional ancillary services at their discretion.

evidence_quotes.quote: Influencer marketing and third-party promotional content does not require new disclosure obligations.

paragraphs.text: Clarify obligations around influencer marketing and commercial content: Influencer marketing and third-party promotional content does not require new disclosure obligations. Current EU law, notably the UCPD and the Audiovisual Media Services Directive (AVMSD), already sets clear requirements on commercial content, hidden advertising, and promotional messaging. Authorities should focus on consistent enforcement to ensure commercial intent is clearly identifiable across all channels.)

evidence_quotes.quote: recurring or membership-based models (e.g., loyalty subscriptions, premium tiers, automatically renewing ancillary bundles).

paragraphs.text: Travel services combine traditional dated, one-off purchases (e.g., flight tickets, holiday packages) with increasingly common recurring or membership-based models (e.g., loyalty subscriptions, premium tiers, automatically renewing ancillary bundles).

section_path: 5.1. Contractual landscape in the travel sector

paragraphs.text: We believe that existing EU consumer law already provides the necessary protections to address these concerns effectively:

section_path: 5.1. Contractual landscape in the travel sector

evidence_quotes.quote: whether a contract will renew automatically
free trials that convert into paid subscriptions
clear and easily accessible cancellation mechanism

paragraphs.text: The Consumer Rights Directive requires providers to give consumers clear, precise, and unambiguous information upfront about the contract duration, renewal terms, and termination procedures. In particular, providers must inform consumers whether a contract will renew automatically and the specific terms of that renewal (e.g., costs, time periods). For free trials that convert into paid subscriptions, the CRD requires contracts to clearly state that a payment obligation will follow the free trial period unless the consumer cancels before it ends. The CRD also mandates the display of a clear and easily accessible cancellation mechanism (such as a prominent button) allowing consumers to terminate a contract or subscription online in just a few steps, without having to navigate hidden menus or contact customer service.

section_path: 5.1. Contractual landscape in the travel sector

evidence_quotes.quote: onerous or disproportionate non-contractual barriers to termination
manipulative interface designs which impede consumers' ability to make free and informed decisions.

paragraphs.text: The UCPD prohibits onerous or disproportionate non-contractual barriers to termination, and the DSA prohibits manipulative interface designs which impede consumers' ability to make free and informed decisions.

section_path: 5.1. Contractual landscape in the travel sector

evidence_quotes.quote: subscription-based models.

paragraphs.text: We believe that these provisions provide sufficient protection for consumers against potential abuses in subscription-based models. The key challenge lies in ensuring consistent enforcement, rather than introducing additional legislation.

section_path: 5.1. Contractual landscape in the travel sector

paragraphs.text: eu travel tech supports the following foundational principles for digital contracts:

evidence_quotes.quote: no more friction in cancelling a digital subscription than they did in signing up

paragraphs.text: Equivalence of entry and exit: The mandate that consumers face no more friction in cancelling a digital subscription than they did in signing up is not a new concept; it is a principle already codified. This expectation is grounded in the Consumer Rights Directive (CRD)'s rules on withdrawal functions and the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD)'s existing prohibition of undue obstacles.

evidence_quotes.quote: Right to human assistance: Where automated support fails on contract-critical issues, consumers must have an accessible and effective channel to contact a human representative if issues cannot be resolved by using automated systems.

paragraphs.text: Right to human assistance: Where automated support fails on contract-critical issues, consumers must have an accessible and effective channel to contact a human representative if issues cannot be resolved by using automated systems.

evidence_quotes.quote: non-refundable dated bookings under the Package Travel Directive and related regimes.

paragraphs.text: Respect for sectoral nuance: Any action in the area should avoid unintentionally undermining justified travel-specific specialities, such as non-refundable dated bookings under the Package Travel Directive and related regimes.
These principles should guide coordinated enforcement and practical guidance, rather than creating new legal obligations.

evidence_quotes.quote: avoid requiring mandatory reminder notices for automatically renewing subscription contracts
avoid mandating cancellation \"at any time with short notice\" since this could discourage discounted long-term offers and reduce consumer choice.

paragraphs.text: Easy digital cancellation: Prioritise enforcement of existing rights, notably the CRD's prominent online withdrawal function and the UCPD's ban on undue obstacles to termination, to ensure straightforward, timely cancellation without adding new obligations. Additionally, avoid requiring mandatory reminder notices for automatically renewing subscription contracts because in short-term subscriptions this requirement can be overwhelming for consumers, since the number of reminder notices could become spam-like and lead to consumer frustration, considering that consumers usually have multiple subscriptions. Also, avoid mandating cancellation "at any time with short notice" since this could discourage discounted long-term offers and reduce consumer choice.

evidence_quotes.quote: Free trials

paragraphs.text: Free trials: Recognise the consumer benefits of free trials, which are already regulated by the CRD/UCPD. Restrictive new measures could reduce availability and choice. Collecting payment at sign-up can legitimately help prevent abuse, verify age, and reinforce awareness of post-trial charges.

evidence_quotes.quote: new subscription-specific rules.

paragraphs.text: Targeted and coordinated enforcement: Ensure consistency across Member States through joint guidance and coordinated sweeps, rather than introducing new subscription-specific rules.

evidence_quotes.quote: personalised pricing notices
ranking and recommender system disclosures

paragraphs.text: Travel tech companies contend with fragmented and overlapping disclosure obligations: pre-contractual information, personalised pricing notices, contract terms, ranking and recommender system disclosures and interface labels often duplicate or contradict one another. Such redundancy confuses consumers and increases compliance costs, especially for cross-border operators navigating diverging national interpretations or "gold-plating" beyond EU minimum standards. It also undermines our ability to provide easy comparison to consumers, threatening our ability to provide transparency to consumers.
The problem is compounded on mobile devices and stems mainly from divergent interpretations and enforcement practices across Member States, rather than from legal gaps.

evidence_quotes.quote: introduce a proportionate pause on new information duties.

paragraphs.text: Regulatory pause to enable continued implementation: Given the recent adoption of new major legal acts (DSA, DMA, AI Act), introduce a proportionate pause on new information duties. This would allow businesses and authorities to focus on implementation of current rules and assess if there are any gaps.

evidence_quotes.quote: progressive disclosure

paragraphs.text: Layered digital delivery: Allow and promote modern, user-centric presentation methods (e.g. expandable summaries, icons, pop-up information windows, progressive disclosure) that present key information upfront while enabling deeper detail on demand. This approach ensures legal completeness without overwhelming users with dense, static text, while improving usability, particularly on mobile interfaces.

evidence_quotes.quote: Eliminate low-value duplication

paragraphs.text: Eliminate low-value duplication: Systematically review existing disclosure obligations to identify and remove redundant or near-duplicate requirements, particularly where identical information is mandated in multiple contexts but slightly different forms. This would streamline consumer information and reduce compliance burdens.
Horizontal issues and enforcement
7.1. The enforcement imperative

Today's divergent approaches across Member States, driven by differences in resources, priorities, and legal interpretation, result in uneven consumer protection, create regulatory safe havens for bad actors, and impose asymmetrical compliance burdens on law-abiding businesses. Rather than creating new legislative layers, the European Commission should work to strengthen coordination between authorities in order to apply the existing robust legal framework (UCPD, DSA, GDPR, CRD, ASR, etc.) consistently across the Single Market. This would reduce fragmentation and close cross-border enforcement gaps.
7.2. New suggested approach
a) Concept of "vulnerable consumer"

evidence_quotes.quote: Altering the established standard of consumer protection, which relies on the concept of a "reasonably well-informed and reasonably observant and circumspect" consumer (i.e., the average consumer), could have significant repercussions for legal certainty, regulatory stability, and the interpretation and enforcement of existing principles and rules.

paragraphs.text: The definitions of "vulnerable" and "average" consumers are well understood and hold significant value as a benchmark for policymaking. Altering the established standard of consumer protection, which relies on the concept of a "reasonably well-informed and reasonably observant and circumspect" consumer (i.e., the average consumer), could have significant repercussions for legal certainty, regulatory stability, and the interpretation and enforcement of existing principles and rules.

evidence_quotes.quote: collection and processing of more personal data to identify at-risk consumers

paragraphs.text: In addition, the notion of vulnerable consumers is already well covered in key EU legal instruments (i.e., GDPR, DSA, Accessibility Act), providing legal stability for companies. Broadening the scope, particularly with vague and subjective terms such as "emotional distress" or "negative mental state" could dilute its impact and clarity. For example, identifying whether a consumer is "vulnerable" in that context could require the collection and processing of more personal data to identify at-risk consumers and could contradict the GDPR data minimisation principle. Instead, the focus should remain on specific at-risk groups, like minors, and be coupled with a risk-based approach that ensures protection, clarity and fairness.
b) Burden of proof and 'fairness by design'

Reversing the burden of proof could lead to increased compliance costs and legal uncertainty. A more effective approach would be to equip authorities with the right tools and sufficient resources to assess compliance and enforce rules. At a time when the EU is seeking ways to boost its competitiveness, it seems counterproductive to push businesses towards a more risk-averse and less innovation-driven mindset at a time of significant transformation.

Similarly, the concept of "fairness by design" is highly generic and could lead to a variety of interpretations by businesses depending on how strictly they approach compliance. Consumer protection (in the form of compliance with existing consumer law and jurisprudence) is already taken into account when developing new products so the added value of introducing a new, standalone duty is unclear. In fact, Article 25 GDPR already includes a "fairness by design" principle. It seems more reasonable to ensure laws include provisions that are clear, implementable, and enforceable, and that they provide legal certainty and predictability.

EU-wide responses: Clarify how existing CPC tools and procedures can be used to coordinate swift EU-wide responses when systemic unfair practices arise in multiple Member States, while avoiding duplication with other existing mechanisms.

evidence_quotes.quote: Formalise joint case-management protocols among consumer protection authorities, DSA national coordinators, data protection authorities, and relevant Commission services (e.g. DG JUST/CNECT/COMP).

paragraphs.text: Inter-regime cooperation: Formalise joint case-management protocols among consumer protection authorities, DSA national coordinators, data protection authorities, and relevant Commission services (e.g. DG JUST/CNECT/COMP). These protocols would align fact-finding, avoid duplicated or conflicting investigations, and harmonise remedies across overlapping legal regimes, leveraging existing cooperation tools rather than creating new ones.

evidence_quotes.quote: Safe harbour protections for businesses that adopt industry best practices.
Safe harbour incentives should remain non-legislative (e.g., Commission-endorsed templates, codes of conduct) and confer a presumption of compliance rather than impose new obligations.

paragraphs.text: Proportionality and safe harbours: Introduce graduated compliance expectations, with safe harbour protections for businesses that adopt industry best practices. Enforcement resources should be prioritised towards persistent, high-impact, or intentionally non-compliant actors. Safe harbour incentives should remain non-legislative (e.g., Commission-endorsed templates, codes of conduct) and confer a presumption of compliance rather than impose new obligations.
EU travel tech and its members underline that the EU already has a comprehensive legal framework to protect consumers in the digital environment.

The priority now is to ensure consistent and coordinated enforcement across the Single Market, coupled with proportionate simplification of existing obligations and boosting the EU's competitiveness in the digital environment. This is the best way to balance economic growth with high consumer protection standards. Proposing new legislation while existing rules remain insufficiently enforced would be premature and counterproductive, especially in light of the EU's broader objective of enhancing its global competitiveness.

eu travel tech remains committed to supporting the Commission by contributing to joint guidance and practical solutions to ensure consumers enjoy fair and transparent digital travel services, while enabling all businesses to compete on equal terms within a well-functioning Single Market.

eu travel tech represents the interests of travel technology companies. eu travel tech uses its position at the centre of the travel and tourism sector to promote a consumer-driven, innovative, and competitive industry that is transparent and sustainable. Our membership spans Global Distribution Systems (GDSs), Online Travel Agencies (OTA), Travel Management Companies in business travel (TMCs) and metasearch sites.

eu travel tech's members include Airbnb, Booking.com, eDreams ODIGEO, etraveli group, Expedia Group and Skyscanner. Associate members include Amadeus, American Express GBT, Awaze, Dohop, GetYourGuide, lastminute.com, Sabre, Trainline, Travelport, Vola and Tripadvisor. Strategic Partners include Idealo and Omio.
The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.
